system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 01:16 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "# Read first 5 and last 5 logs from each case to capture key patterns
for slug in michael-deshields lindsey-dunn lori-house michael-ditto-jr collie-williams-sr collieon-williams jordan-brown jensen-ott aleesha-williams michae-guyton christopher-lanier; do
  echo "============ $slug ============"
  logdir="/opt/data/FirmVault/cases/$slug/Activity Log"
  files=$(ls -1 "$logdir"/*.md 2>/dev/null | sort)
  
  # First 5
  for f in $(echo "$files" | head -5); do
    echo "--- $(basename "$f") ---"
    head -30 "$f"
    echo "..."
  done
  
  # Last 5
  for f in $(echo "$files" | tail -5); do
    echo "--- $(basename "$f") ---"
    head -30 "$f"
    echo "..."
  done
  echo ""
done", "timeout": 60}

The space in "Activity Log" is causing issues. Let me use proper quoting:

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault
# Read sample logs using find to handle spaces
for slug in michael-deshields lindsey-dunn lori-house michael-ditto-jr collie-williams-sr collieon-williams jordan-brown jensen-ott aleesha-williams michae-guyton christopher-lanier; do
  echo "============ $slug ============"
  logdir="cases/$slug/Activity Log"
  
  # Get first 5 files
  echo "--- FIRST 5 ---"
  find "$logdir" -name "*.md" -type f | sort | head -5 | while IFS= read -r f; do
    echo ">>> $(basename "$f")"
    head -40 "$f"
    echo "---END---"
  done
  
  # Get last 5 files
  echo "--- LAST 5 ---"
  find "$logdir" -name "*.md" -type f | sort | tail -5 | while IFS= read -r f; do
    echo ">>> $(basename "$f")"
    head -40 "$f"
    echo "---END---"
  done
done 2>&1 | head -3000", "timeout": 60}

   292|### Key New Patterns Identified

   298|- **SOL alerts run on closed cases**: Filevine's automated SOL countdown does not account for case status — alerts continue even after a case is declined.
   299|- **4+ year litigation cases exist**: Multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers can extend far beyond the typical 2-3 year PI case lifecycle.

   346|### Key New Patterns Identified
   347|- **UIM claims require a separate litigation track**: When BI limits are insufficient, UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer in a separate court, with separate discovery and defense counsel
   348|- **Coverage denial creates the longest case timelines**: When a BI carrier denies coverage (stolen vehicle), the case can extend 4+ years with coverage investigation, EUOs, and forced litigation
   349|- **Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) is the fallback PIP mechanism**: When no BI coverage exists, KAC assigns a carrier for PIP benefits — but the process involves negotiation about which bills are covered
   350|- **Medicaid liens (Conduent) are actively monitored**: Unlike passive private insurer liens, Conduent calls the firm to check on case status — and Medicaid liens can be the largest single deduction
   351|- **Dental injuries create unique timeline pressure**: Staged implant procedures (18+ months) push cases toward the SOL, often requiring litigation filing before treatment is complete
   352|- **Same carrier for multiple claim types creates confusion**: When PIP, BI, or UIM are at the same carrier, different adjusters handle each track — but the firm must carefully distinguish which claim it's communicating about
   353|- **Pre-settlement funding of $31K+ can exceed net recovery**: In complex cases with large liens and extensive treatment, pre-settlement funding can consume most or all of the client's share

   458|### Key New Patterns Identified
   459|- **Wrongful death cases fundamentally alter the case lifecycle**: No treatment, no records collection, no demand — the case goes directly from intake to investigation and litigation. The PHASE_DAG must account for this exception.
   460|- **Third-party claims administrators add complexity to PL cases**: Large retailers use third-party claims management (not their own insurer), creating an additional layer of communication.
   461|- **WC estate claims face immediate procedural challenges**: Claims filed in a deceased individual's name are denied on sight — the Motion to Substitute Plaintiff is a critical first step.
   462|- **WC cases can take 5+ years**: The estate WC case (DOI 12/2020, Board opinion 2/2025) is the longest observed case timeline, confirming that WC administrative proceedings are slower than even complex PI litigation.
   463|- **Multi-plaintiff cross-state filings add jurisdictional complexity**: Filing an MVA case in Indiana when the firm is KY-based requires coordination across state court systems.
   464|- **Certified records billing errors occur**: Providers can certify incorrect billing amounts, requiring audit and correction correspondence before records can be used in litigation.
   465|- **Authorization form rejections are a recurring bottleneck**: Missing signature dates, missing DOB on authorization forms causes provider rejection and adds round-trip delay.
   466|- **Settlement release negotiations in wrongful death cases span months**: Multiple defense counsel, Medicare considerations, and large liens ($171K+ WC lien) create extended resolution timelines.
   467|
   468|### Gaps Partially Addressed
   469|- **Wrongful death case patterns**: First case provides initial framework; need more for confidence
   470|- **WC estate claims**: Second WC case provides full administrative resolution lifecycle (first to reach Board opinion)
   471|- **Cross-state litigation**: First IN filing observed; need more to understand procedural differences
   472|
   473|### Remaining Gaps
   474|- Wrongful death settlement disbursement: Case still open
   475|- WC Board appeal process: Unknown what happens if Board opinion is appealed
   476|- Cross-state service of process: How defendants are served in different jurisdictions
   477|- Wrongful death damages calculation: No settlement values observed for comparison
   478|- Third-party claims administrator negotiation tactics: Only one case with TPA observed

   486|- Case N (premises liability, litigation phase): 374 activity logs — KY PL estate case (DOI 7/14/2020, fall at hospital), injured client died, surviving spouse as estate representative, complaint filed Jefferson Circuit Court (21-CI-003798), 5+ year litigation, full discovery (IROGs, RFAs, document production via e-filing and Dropbox), expert consultant retained, home health provider misidentified (CareTenders vs Kindred at Home), Humana lien ($5,887.97), probate inventory completed, referred to outside counsel at one point

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Intake and Onboarding Workflow
    18|
    19|> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Standard intake workflow observed across cases:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
    26|2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
    27|3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
    28|4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
    29|5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
    30|6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.
    31|7. **Provider Setup**: Add all medical providers to the system. Send records/billing requests.
    32|8. **Phase Change**: Case moves from File Setup to Treatment.
    33|
    34|Timeline: This entire workflow typically spans 3-7 days from initial contact.
    35|
    36|## Evidence
    37|
    38|- In Case B, the initial contact came from the client's husband who provided detailed accident information, insurance claim numbers, and adjuster contacts. The firm requested personal info (DOB, SSN, etc.), sent VineSign intake docs, and received signed docs within 3 days.
    39|- In Case C, the intake and welcome call happened on day 1 (4/25/2024). Insurance claims were set up and LORs sent the same day. Records requests went out by 4/29.
    40|- In Case A, intake was complicated by the prior firm transfer — mom signed intake docs via VineSign, but the file transfer from the prior firm took over a week.
    41|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), the BI adjuster proactively called the firm looking for the LOR the day after the accident report was filed. LORs were sent to both BI and PIP within 1 day. The provider discovery process was incremental — the client provided some providers on day 1, then additional providers were identified over subsequent calls (TN hospital, TX orthopedic, concussion specialist). This iterative provider discovery extended across weeks. A second accident (occurring one month after the first) was identified during intake, requiring separate claim setup for an NYC cab company (American Transit Insurance).
    42|- In Case F (WC/other case, DOI 12/2022), intake was handled through CaseOpp system. The CaseOpp used the wrong contract type (PI instead of WC), requiring a re-send of WC-specific intake forms. The client's VM was not set up, requiring callback. DocuSign intake docs were viewed multiple times before completion — client reported difficulty with a required field. Despite multiple views, completion took 7 days from first send. Provider identification required a client callback where treatment history was confirmed (ER, orthopedic surgeon, PT).
    43|- In Case G (MVA, DOI 4/2023), intake docs were sent via VineSign on day 1. The welcome call revealed the client had a companion case (daughter) — her file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. Multiple attempts to reach the client failed (VM full), with the firm pivoting to SMS. SSN and health insurance card were not obtained during initial intake and required 3+ follow-up attempts over 2 weeks. The companion case pattern added complexity to intake.
    44|- In Case H (MVA, DOI 12/2022), intake came via a witness/referral (Robert Grant). The firm was unable to reach the client directly — both phone numbers resulted in no answer and no VM. The police report number was obtained from the witness. Eventually the firm reached the client through the witness. This pattern shows that difficult-to-reach clients require alternative contact strategies from the start.
    45|- In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.
    46|- In Case J (MVA, DOI 1/2023, UM claim), intake triggered immediate SSN request and welcome call. Client was unreachable on first attempt; VM left. Contact via text/email was initiated same day. Two-week follow-up cycle started. Client eventually responded via text. Provider setup and insurance claims (BI + UM + PIP to same carrier) done within 4 days.
    47|- In Case K (trucking MVA, DOI 2/2024), intake docs were scanned and forwarded by managing attorney. Client phone number was non-functional on first call attempt — staff pivoted to text. Client eventually reached via alternate number. No health insurance. File Setup phase change occurred same day as intake.
    48|- In Case L (MVA, DOI 1/2023, multi-carrier), intake was rapid — records request to hospital sent via ChartSwap on day 1 after intake. LOR sent to both BI carriers (KFB and Progressive) within 24 hours. The client contacted firm about financial hardship (cancelled medical appointments, no transportation) within 6 days — highlighting how intake must set timeline expectations early.
    49|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2024), client was spoken to briefly — she was running errands. She preferred text messages. Firm immediately pivoted to text-based communication. Police report number and health insurance card were requested via text. Records requests sent same day.
    50|- In Case N (WC+BI, DOI 6/2021, transferred from TN attorney), intake was emergency — SOL was the next day. Complaint was filed on the day of intake before any records or provider setup. This case skipped normal onboarding entirely; the firm went straight to litigation.
    51|- In Case O (MVA, DOI 5/2024), intake docs sent via VineSign on day 1. Attorney flagged that 5 years of prior medical records were needed from all providers (not just accident records). This represents a higher documentation requirement for demand preparation.
    52|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), the standard VineSign intake workflow was confirmed in every case: intake sent via VineSign, signed within 1-3 days, followed by welcome call/text, then phase change to File Setup. In one slip-and-fall case, intake was processed alongside insurance claim setup within the same week. In 2 cases, the welcome call task was auto-generated ("Contact [Client] and do Welcome Call and send Welcome Email(if able). Confirm treatment providers and enter any in system that have not been entered.") — confirming this is a standardized template task. One case involved a client with only a minor-care-clinic visit (single $99 bill), showing that even minimal-treatment cases follow the same intake workflow. In a case involving a Spanish-named client from an out-of-state accident (Indiana), the intake included a rental car agreement forwarded to the file, showing clients proactively providing documentation. In 3 cases, companion cases (same-accident family members) shared intake timing: two companions from the same MVA had overlapping file setup and insurance claim creation on the same day.
    53|
    54|## Agent Guidance
    55|
    56|1. Request all necessary information in the first communication (don't spread it across multiple contacts)
    57|2. Send VineSign intake docs within 24 hours of initial contact
    58|3. If docs are not signed within 48 hours, follow up
    59|4. Conduct the welcome call within 24 hours of signed docs
    60|5. Set up insurance claims (LORs to BI and PIP) within 48 hours of signed docs
    61|6. Identify and add all medical providers within the first week
    62|7. Log the phase change from Lead to File Setup once docs are signed
    63|8. For referral cases (husband calling for wife, etc.), get the actual client to sign the docs
    64|
    65|## Pitfalls
    66|
    67|- Slow VineSign turnaround — follow up if not signed within 48 hours
    68|- Playing phone tag with the client for days during intake (use text/email as backup)
    69|- Not capturing all insurance information (both BI and PIP carriers) in the initial contact
    70|- Not asking about all medical providers during the welcome call
    71|- Not sending LORs promptly (this delays adjuster acknowledgment and can delay everything downstream)
    72|
    73|## Related
    74|
    75|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — intake triggers the first phase transitions
    76|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — intake often involves a family member
    77|- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — transfer cases have a modified intake workflow
    78|", "total_lines": 77, "file_size": 9329, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/medical-malpractice-case-patterns.md", "content": "---
title: Medical Malpractice Case Patterns
slug: medical-malpractice-case-patterns
phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment]
category: edge-cases
tags: [medical-malpractice, med-mal, minor-client, hospital-records, pediatric, birth-injury]
confidence: low
evidence_count: 1
created: 2026-04-12
updated: 2026-04-12
related:
  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
  - medical-records-collection
  - client-communication-through-family
  - lien-management-health-insurance
---

# Medical Malpractice Case Patterns

> Medical malpractice cases differ fundamentally from auto accident PI cases in case setup, provider relationships, record collection, and insurance dynamics. The at-fault party IS the medical provider, which means the standard BI/PIP insurance framework does not apply. Instead, the firm pursues a claim against the provider's malpractice insurance. These cases often involve minors as the injured party with a parent/guardian as the primary contact.

## Pattern

Key differences from auto accident cases observed:

1. **No BI/PIP Insurance Framework**: Unlike auto accidents, there are no BI or PIP claims to file. The case targets the provider's professional liability (malpractice) coverage. Insurance claims setup is marked as not applicable.
2. **Provider Is Both Treater and Defendant**: The medical provider whose records are needed is also the defendant. This creates a unique dynamic where records requests go to the party being sued.
3. **Minor Client with Guardian Contact**: The observed case involved a minor (pediatric patient) with a parent as the primary contact and signatory on all documents.
4. **Specialized Provider Types**: Treatment involves pediatric specialists (children's hospital, pediatric urology, women's/children's hospital) rather than the chiropractic/ER/urgent care providers common in auto cases.
5. **Health Insurance Lien Present**: The minor's health insurer (in the observed case, a national carrier) has a lien on any recovery, similar to auto accident cases.
6. **Extended Timeline**: The observed case had a DOI over 2 years before the last activity, with the case still in treatment phase — suggesting med mal cases have longer pre-demand timelines.
7. **All Records Received Early**: Records and bills were received from all providers within approximately one month of the request, faster than the typical auto accident case. This may be because hospital systems have more structured records departments.

## Evidence

- In one case involving a pediatric medical malpractice claim (DOI in spring 2023, KY jurisdiction), the minor was treated at a children's hospital system with multiple departments (medical group, urology, women's and children's hospital). Records from all three departments were received on the same day approximately 5 weeks after intake. A national health insurer lien was identified. No BI or PIP claims were filed. The parent served as primary contact. Case was still in treatment phase nearly 2 years post-DOI. Activity volume was low (45 logs) compared to auto accident cases of similar age, suggesting a monitoring posture.

## Agent Guidance

1. Do NOT attempt to set up BI or PIP claims for medical malpractice cases — there are no such claims
2. Verify the minor's guardian/parent has legal authority to sign intake documents
3. Records requests go to the defendant provider — handle with awareness that these records may later be contested
4. Identify health insurance liens early, as they apply the same way as in auto accident cases
5. Expect a longer pre-demand timeline — med mal cases require expert review before demand
6. Track the statute of limitations carefully — med mal SOL rules differ from auto accident SOL in most jurisdictions

## Pitfalls

- Attempting to file BI/PIP claims when none apply
- Not identifying that the treating provider IS the defendant early enough
- Missing the different SOL rules for medical malpractice vs. auto accident cases
- Not establishing guardian/parent communication protocols from intake

## Related

- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — med mal cases require modified intake (no insurance claims)
- [[medical-records-collection]] — records come from the defendant provider
- [[client-communication-through-family]] — minor clients require parent/guardian as contact
- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — health insurance liens apply to med mal recoveries
"}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/pip-exhaustion-triggers-provider-billing-pressure.md", "content": "---
title: PIP Exhaustion Triggers Provider Billing Pressure
slug: pip-exhaustion-triggers-provider-billing-pressure
phase: [phase_2_treatment, phase_3_demand]

  - medical-bill-collections-anxiety

# PIP Exhaustion Triggers Provider Billing Pressure

> This connection documents how [[pip-claims-management]] exhaustion creates immediate billing pressure from medical providers, which cascades into urgency in [[demand-preparation-workflow]]. When PIP benefits run out, providers who previously billed PIP directly begin contacting the firm about outstanding balances, requesting PIP logs, and asking whether "more PIP is available or no funds till settlement."

## Pattern

The sequence observed across multiple cases:

1. Client receives treatment; provider bills PIP carrier directly
2. PIP benefits exhaust (the $10,000 KY PIP limit is reached)
3. Provider discovers PIP is exhausted (either from denied payment or by requesting a PIP log)
4. Provider contacts the firm asking about remaining PIP and whether to hold the balance "till settlement"
5. Firm sends PIP exhaust letter to the provider confirming PIP is exhausted
6. Provider balance becomes a lien-like obligation that must be addressed in the demand/settlement
7. This creates urgency to complete the demand and resolve the BI claim

This pattern is particularly common with urgent care and chiropractic providers who operate on a letter-of-protection model. The provider's front desk staff (not billing departments) often initiate these inquiries via email, creating informal communication threads.

## Evidence

- In one case, an urgent care clinic contacted the firm: "The above-mentioned patient has a balance of [amount]. Please advise if more PIP is available or no funds till settlement. We also are requesting a PIP log." The firm responded: "PIP exhausted. STILL NEED HIS MEDICAL RECORDS. Have bill. Exhaust letter attached." This shows the PIP exhaustion moment also becomes a records-collection touchpoint.
- In a second case with the same urgent care clinic, the provider sent an identical inquiry template for a different client: "[amount] balance. Please advise if more PIP is available or no funds till settlement." The firm responded: "Only provider. Please resubmit billing. Plenty of PIP remaining." — showing that not all provider billing inquiries indicate PIP exhaustion; some are routine balance checks even when PIP is available.
- In a third case, an adjuster requested additional records after reviewing the PIP log, noting that "what PIP has paid is significantly higher than the records from the demand show." This demonstrates that PIP payment amounts visible in the log can create evidentiary discrepancies that adjusters exploit during negotiation.

## Agent Guidance

1. When PIP approaches exhaustion, proactively notify providers and send exhaust letters
2. Use the PIP exhaustion moment to simultaneously request outstanding records/bills from the provider
3. Track provider balance inquiries as a signal that PIP has exhausted or is about to
4. Maintain a standardized PIP exhaust letter template for rapid response
5. When PIP is NOT exhausted but providers inquire, redirect billing to the PIP carrier with updated claim information

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP exhaustion is the trigger event
- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — provider balance inquiries can escalate to collections if unresolved
- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — PIP exhaustion creates urgency to complete the demand
"}

Now let me add evidence to the key updated articles. Let me add evidence entries to the articles that need substantive content additions:

{"content": "    30|## Evidence
    31|
    32|- In Case G (MVA), the client's daughter was identified as a companion case during the welcome call. Her Filevine file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. The primary client was asked repeatedly over 6+ months to obtain Children's Hospital records for the companion but failed to do so. Both cases were joined in one lawsuit (Boone 24-CI-00452). During mediation via Zoom, both cases received separate LBF (last best & final) offers. Mediation failed for both.
    33|- In Case I (MVA), a companion witness (Dana Jackson) was deposed alongside the client. Depositions were scheduled sequentially via Zoom — the client was told "we are going to do Dana's deposition first so just chill and I will shoot you a text when we are about done." Coordinating two depositions on the same day required active text communication to manage timing.
    34|- In 2 cases from this batch, companion cases were identified at intake: one MVA had a driver and passenger (mother/son) treated at the same ER, creating parallel files with shared BI carrier but separate demand tracks. The paralegal sent demands referencing both clients' records to the same BI adjuster. In another, a companion case was noted but the intake was sent to the companion "via vinesign" through a family member contact. Companion cases with shared chiropractic providers can have records/billing returned together, requiring manual separation.
    35|- In an MVA case (multi-plaintiff, Floyd County IN), three plaintiffs from the same accident were joined in one lawsuit. The complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action. Deposition scheduling required coordination across all plaintiffs through court reporting services. Mediation was being scheduled for all cases simultaneously. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN), adding jurisdictional complexity for companion case coordination. Two of the settled BI claims were resolved separately per plaintiff, confirming that settlement amounts are independent even in joint litigation.
    36|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), three additional passengers in the same vehicle were each represented by different law firms (4 total plaintiffs). The at-fault driver's insurance tendered policy limits (25/50) to be divided among all four plaintiffs. The coordination challenge shifted from records collection to inter-counsel distribution: the firm had to email all three other firms to negotiate allocation. This process stalled for months (Jan-Apr 2024+) as counsel for one co-plaintiff was slow to provide medical bills needed for allocation. The delay frustrated the client, who called repeatedly asking for updates and was told "we are still waiting on a reply on the one person we're waiting for medical bills." When distribution could not be agreed upon, the case moved to litigation with all plaintiffs joined in one lawsuit. Depositions for multiple plaintiffs in the joint suit were coordinated through defense counsel.
    37|- In a bus accident case (DOI 7/2024), two passengers (companions) were injured in the same incident. Both were filed as separate cases but shared the same defendant (commercial transportation company + unknown driver) and the same insurance carrier. Settlement demands were sent for both companions simultaneously. Defense counsel corresponded about both clients in single email threads (\"Abby Sitgraves & Nayram Adadevoh\"). The companion filing was confirmed as \"et al\" in the complaint (Jefferson 25-CI-000133). Each companion had independent medical providers and separate health insurance liens. The out-of-state companion's records collection required different providers than the primary client.
    38|- In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion relationship was between spouses. The complaint was filed as a companion case in the same jurisdiction. Both clients shared PIP coverage but had separate BI claims. The firm tracked SOL independently for each companion.
    39|- In an MVA case (negotiation phase), a non-English-speaking client had a companion case that was not yet uploaded. The case manager noted they would "wait for the companions case to be uploaded and will make it as the point of contact," indicating that the companion case setup was blocking the primary's file setup. The companion case was referenced by a co-counsel attorney negotiating jointly, with three attorneys (from three firms) coordinating a multi-party negotiation with the same BI carrier. This shows that companion cases can involve inter-firm coordination, not just intra-firm coordination.
    40|- In an MVA case (litigation phase), three passengers from the same vehicle (client plus two companions) were represented by the same firm in a multi-plaintiff action filed in Floyd County. The lawsuit was styled as "et al. v. [Defendant]" and depositions were coordinated across all plaintiffs. Multiple BI carriers and a PIP carrier were involved, with each companion having independent claims against potentially different carriers. The case confirmed that companion case mediation scheduling requires all plaintiffs' availability.
    41|- In an MVA case (closed/settlement), two spouses were injured and filed companion cases against the same BI carrier, with shared PIP and UIM coverage through the same insurer. The firm sent records/billing for both clients to the BI adjuster in the same communication, and followed up on offers for both simultaneously. The BI claim settled first, triggering a COOTS letter, while the UIM track continued separately. This demonstrates the companion case pattern where one claim type settles while others remain open across the same companion set.
    42|- In an MVA case (treatment phase), the case involved 3 separate BI carriers (two at-fault drivers plus a third carrier), with liability accepted by one carrier while negotiations continued with others. The multi-carrier structure meant that each companion's recovery depended on different liability determinations from different carriers, adding complexity beyond typical companion cases that share a single defendant.
    43|- In 3 companion case pairs from this batch (DOIs 12/2025 and 6/2025), companion cases shared the same accident, same BI carrier (Progressive), and same PIP carrier (Kentucky Farm Bureau) with the same claim numbers and adjusters. Each companion had independent medical providers — one companion treated at a physical therapy clinic while the other had orthopedic treatment. File setup for both companions occurred on the same day with overlapping insurance claim creation. This confirms that companion cases with shared carriers should have simultaneous file setup to avoid delays, and that PIP claims can share a single claim number across companions.
    44|
    45|## Agent Guidance
    46|
    47|1. During intake, ask whether other family members were in the vehicle and injured
    48|2. Create separate case files for each companion immediately — do not delay
    49|3. Send intake documents to companions as early as possible
    50|4. Track records collection for each companion independently
    51|5. When the primary client is the gateway to companion information, set explicit deadlines and follow up
    52|6. For litigation, coordinate with the attorney on whether to file jointly or separately
    53|7. Schedule depositions for companions on the same day when possible to minimize coordination burden
    54|8. During mediation, prepare separate settlement evaluations for each companion
    55|
    56|## Pitfalls
    57|
    58|- Discovering companion cases days/weeks after the primary intake (records requests are delayed)
    59|- In Case R (auto accident, DOI 4/2023), the client was identified as a companion case at intake — a note explicitly stated "Companion: [family member]" with a link to the related Filevine project. Both clients were joined in the same lawsuit. Depositions were scheduled separately for both parties at the same defense counsel's office. The companion's case had a distinct demand amount. Both depositions included the defendant, confirming that companion case litigation coordinates plaintiff, companion, and defendant depositions. The case also demonstrates that companion cases may require separate demand evaluations (the companion's demand was for $5K on minor injuries while the related case could have a different amount).
    60|- Relying on the primary client to obtain companion's information without explicit deadlines
    61|- Not creating a separate case file (tracking companion in notes on primary case)
    62|- Not requesting companion's medical records independently (assuming they're covered by the primary request)
    63|- Scheduling conflicts when companions need to attend the same deposition/mediation session
    64|
    65|- In a multi-passenger MVA case (DOI 11/2025), four family members (two adults, two minor children) were involved in a rear-end collision. The BI and PIP claims shared claim numbers across all companions. The PIP adjuster (at Progressive) handled all four claimants in a single email thread, completing coverage investigation and issuing individualized payment decisions per claimant. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as "proof of loss" for the minors' PIP claims. This confirms that companion cases with minor children require adult passengers to provide statements as proxy evidence. The PIP adjuster denied specific billing codes differently per companion (different denied CPT codes for each child vs. each adult), demonstrating that even within the same accident, PIP payment analysis is individualized per claimant.
    66|- In the same case, the companion relationship was parent-children: one parent's phone number served as the contact for all four cases, and the firm used that single number for all communication. Reservation of Rights letters were issued for the minors but not the adults, creating a split coverage status across companions from the same accident.
    67|
    68|## Related
    69|
    70|", "total_lines": 72, "file_size": 11816, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=70 to continue reading (showing 30-69 of 72 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Records and bills were collected independently per companion, and both reached "all records received" status around the same time. Both shared a PIP carrier and the same BI carrier (Root Auto Insurance). During negotiation, the BI adjuster reviewed the PIP log for the daughter and found that "what PIP has paid is significantly higher than the records from the demand show," requesting additional records. The adjuster then sent "increased offers for your clients" (plural), indicating that companion case offer adjustments can be communicated in a single thread. This confirms the pattern that companion cases sharing a BI adjuster may receive coordinated offer updates.

## Related"}

{"content": "    90|4. When a client shows Stage 2 behavior, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    91|5. Never tell a client the case is "on settlement" unless the client has already agreed to the amount
    92|6. After every client contact, set the next follow-up date in the system
    93|7. Document all contact attempts — even missed calls — in the activity log
    94|8. When multiple missed calls from one client occur in a day, treat it as an urgent response needed
    95|
    96|## Pitfalls
    97|
    98|- Letting client calls go unreturned for days/weeks
    99|- Client voicemail being full (preventing you from leaving a message) without pivoting to text
   100|- Staff saying "I'm not sure on status" when client calls — everyone on the case should know the current status
   101|- Responding to frustration with vague reassurances instead of specific next steps
   102|- Not escalating frustrated clients to the attorney
   103|- Allowing the pattern to reach Stage 4 before taking action
   104|
   105|## Related
   106|
   107|- [[client-expectations-management]] — proactive management prevents this escalation
   108|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase is when frustration peaks
   109|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — frustrated clients should be escalated to attorney
   110|", "total_lines": 109, "file_size": 15152, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "    70|- In Case H, client communication was relayed through "Anna per Client instructions" — the intermediary called for updates when the client felt out of the loop. After Bryce Koon's departure from the firm, the case was reassigned, and the client needed to be re-introduced to the new attorney — a staff transition that exacerbated the frustration pattern.
    71|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), the client called multiple staff members repeatedly during active litigation (6/2025-3/2026): missed calls to the associate attorney, then to the lead attorney, then to the paralegal — all on the same day (3/3/2026). The voicemail was forwarded internally, and the attorney returned the call the same day. This demonstrates that even when substantive litigation activity is ongoing (depositions being scheduled), clients still escalate if they are not proactively informed.
    72|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), the client showed Stage 1 behavior at 6/2025 (calling about documents she expected but hadn't received), followed by steady escalation with missed calls to multiple staff members (7/2025, 8/2025 — 3 missed calls in 8 minutes on 8/22/2025), continuing through 12/2025-1/2026. The case had $31K in pre-settlement funding creating additional financial pressure.
    73|- In Case H (auto accident, 4+ year litigation), the client texted "Good morning was reaching out for an update" (3/2025) and "Hello was wondering if we had an update on our case haven't heard anything since March" (5/2025). The firm responded within a day with specific information about deposition scheduling. This shows that proactive updates after court events prevent escalation.
    74|- In a workers' compensation case (workplace injury, treatment phase), the client sent emails with increasingly urgent subject lines: "ANY UPDATES?" (8/2025) and "UPDATES??" (10/2025). The escalating punctuation and capitalization follow the same trajectory as the phone-based pattern. The firm responded to each, but the repeated pattern over months confirms that even cases in active treatment (not just negotiation) can trigger client frustration when the client perceives a lack of forward progress. The client also asked about deposition scheduling details and case status, suggesting the frustration was compounded by not understanding the WC litigation process.
    75|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, negotiation/litigation), the client's frustration followed a textbook escalation: (1) polite inquiry phase with regular updates from the firm (Aug-Dec 2023), (2) frustration peaked when the client was told "we are still waiting on [another plaintiff's] medical bills" repeatedly over months (Jan-Apr 2024), (3) the client called to complain about "not receiving call backs or being told conflicting information repeatedly" — she came to the office in person to demand an update (Feb 2024), (4) the attorney spoke with her directly and calmed the situation. The pattern continued in the litigation phase with multiple missed calls (Sep-Nov 2025), text message difficulties ("Can u you give me a call it won't lemme call you"), and the attorney texting back and forth to coordinate. This case uniquely shows frustration driven by inter-counsel coordination delays — the client's case was ready but held hostage by another firm's inaction.
    76|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023, transferred from prior counsel), the client made 20+ missed calls to the attorney over a 7-month period (Aug 2025 - Mar 2026). The attorney returned calls periodically but the ratio was approximately 5:1 client calls to attorney callbacks. The pattern was remarkably persistent — multiple calls per week with no voicemail content logged, suggesting either hang-ups or voicemail not recorded. The client's frustration is compounded by the case being transferred from prior counsel (already frustrated) and the complex litigation (multiple defendants, out-of-state service).
    77|- In a dog bite case involving a minor (DOI 8/2022), the guardian (mother) made regular missed calls to both the associate and lead attorney. After the assigned associate attorney changed (case handoff to new attorney), missed calls increased. The guardian called the lead attorney directly when the associate was unresponsive. Depositions were cancelled and rescheduled multiple times by defense counsel, adding to guardian frustration about case progress. Despite positive deposition outcome ("went very well"), the communication gap continued.
    78|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client called repeatedly (missed calls to both paralegal and attorney) but the client's own voicemail was full, preventing the firm from leaving messages. The communication breakdown was bidirectional — neither party could reach the other effectively. The firm noted "voicemail full" when trying to call back.
    79|- In a WC case (DOI 5/2023), the client had regular communication through treatment monitoring, but the case shifted dramatically when the firm learned of the client's death via the client's mother's voicemail. This extreme case demonstrates that communication patterns can change instantly and family members may be the first to notify the firm of critical events.
    80|- In Case N (premises liability, estate case, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called for updates repeatedly throughout 2025-2026. The pattern showed: July 2025, the spouse called saying he "wants to speak with attorney" and "has not rec'd a return call." The firm provided an update via the paralegal. Missed calls continued through Sep 2025, Oct 2025 (two missed calls same day), Dec 2025 (two more missed calls), and Mar 2026. This demonstrates that estate cases, where the primary contact is a surviving family member rather than the injured party, follow the same frustration escalation. The emotional dimension is heightened — the spouse is dealing with both grief and the legal process. The 5+ year timeline makes sustained communication even more critical.
    81|- In Case T (premises liability, DOI 9/2023), the client made repeated missed calls to the attorney over a concentrated period (Feb-Mar 2026) — 7+ missed calls in approximately 6 weeks. The calls came in pairs (back-to-back within minutes), suggesting the client was calling, not getting through, and immediately redialing. The case was in active negotiation with defense counsel during this period, confirming that clients escalate even when the case IS moving — the issue is the client doesn't know it's moving.
    82|- In Case U (auto accident, employer liability/taxi company, DOI 9/2024), the client made an extraordinary number of missed calls — 8+ in the last 6 weeks of the activity log alone (Jan-Mar 2026), including back-to-back calls within minutes. The case involved bifurcated litigation issues and a Medicare lien, adding complexity. The client's frustration is notable because the case was relatively recent (DOI just 17 months prior), suggesting that litigation complexity and Medicare bureaucracy trigger faster frustration escalation even on newer cases.
    83|- In Case V (auto accident, closed case, DOI 12/2024), the client made multiple missed calls in September 2025 and continued calling even after the settlement phase. The client expressed frustration about still receiving medical bills despite the case progressing, asking the firm to help with bill payment. The firm told the client to pay bills and submit receipts for reimbursement from PIP. This shows a unique frustration trigger: clients who are being contacted by bill collectors while their case is in settlement/disbursement — the frustration is not about case progress but about the tangible financial pain of unpaid bills.
    84|
    85|## Agent Guidance
    86|
    87|1. Set automated 2-week check-in reminders for every active case in demand/negotiation phase
    88|2. When a client calls and you cannot provide an update, say WHEN they can expect one (e.g., "I will have an update by Friday")
    89|3. If you cannot reach the client (their voicemail is full or not set up), send a text immediately
    90|", "total_lines": 109, "file_size": 15152, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=90 to continue reading (showing 70-89 of 109 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Now add evidence to pip-claims-management for the PIP exhaustion and no-PIP patterns:

{"content": "    55|- In a case with no PIP coverage available ("NO TREATMENT TO DATE AND NO PIP"), the BI claim proceeded independently without any PIP track. This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
    56|- In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
    57|- In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
    58|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.
    59|
    60|## Agent Guidance
    61|
    62|1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
    63|2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
    64|3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
    65|4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
    66|5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
    67|6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
    68|7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
    69|8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP
    70|
    71|## Pitfalls
    72|
    73|- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
    74|- Sending bills once and not following up for months
    75|- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
    76|- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
    77|- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction
    78|
    79|## Related

{"success": true, "diff": "--- a//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/pip-claims-management.md
+++ b//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/pip-claims-management.md
@@ -55,6 +55,8 @@
 - In a case with no PIP coverage available ("NO TREATMENT TO DATE AND NO PIP"), the BI claim proceeded independently without any PIP track. This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
 - In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
 - In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
+- In 8 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-2/2026), PIP management was observed across State Farm (4 cases), Progressive (1), Liberty Mutual (1), National General (1), and Root Auto Insurance (1). Key observations: (1) PIP exhaustion triggers provider billing inquiries — in one case, a chiropractic/urgent care provider emailed requesting a PIP log and asking "please advise if more PIP is available or no funds till settlement"; the firm responded with a PIP exhaust letter; (2) In one case, PIP was confirmed as "plenty of PIP remaining" and the firm instructed the provider to resubmit billing directly to PIP; (3) State Farm PIP was managed through a centralized PIP team (separate from the BI adjuster) accessible via a dedicated phone extension; (4) One case with Root Auto Insurance as PIP carrier and a different carrier for BI confirmed the pattern of split-carrier PIP/BI management; (5) In one case, the PIP carrier (Progressive) requested recorded statements as part of a "proof of loss" investigation for PIP claims, scheduling it through the firm — this is distinct from the BI recorded statement and was handled as a separate event.
+- In one case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "NO PIP COVERAGE" at intake on the same day. The case proceeded with BI only (Travelers Insurance). This confirms that not all KY auto accident cases have PIP, and the coverage determination must be made immediately at intake. This is the second observed no-PIP case in the wiki.
 - Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

{"content": "    60|- In cases with client at fault (1 case) and no BI/UM coverage (1 case), demand was never prepared — the case was declined before reaching that phase. This confirms that demand preparation requires viable BI or UM recovery path.
    61|- In an MVA case (DOI 7/2025), the demand workflow proceeded rapidly: treatment complete, records/bills collected from 4 providers, PIP ledger requested, and BI carrier tendered policy limits ($25K). The firm then transitioned to the UM claim track, sending a COOTS letter. The demand-to-settlement on the BI track was completed in approximately 8 months from DOI, which is among the faster timelines observed for a case with 4 providers.
    62|- In a demand-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the firm sent demand materials directly to a third-party claims administrator (CCMSI) rather than a traditional insurance carrier. The demand requested policy limits tender. When the initial adjuster didn't respond, the demand was re-sent to a different adjuster at the same TPA. This confirms that TPA-managed BI claims follow the same demand workflow but may require adjuster escalation within the TPA structure.
    63|- In a slip-and-fall demand case (DOI 4/2025), the demand was gated on records from a specialist (orthopedic) that the firm was having difficulty obtaining despite multiple fax requests over months. The entry "having issues obtaining norton ortho records. resending requests" illustrates how a single provider's non-responsiveness can block the entire demand timeline.
    64|- In an MVA demand case (DOI 12/2025), the firm requested a PIP ledger and provided it to the treatment provider along with the message "Ledger attached. 7k paid to [provider]. Please resubmit billing." This confirms the PIP-ledger-to-provider coordination step before demand: providers need to know what PIP has already paid so they can adjust their billing, which then affects the demand amount calculation.
    65|
    66|## Agent Guidance
    67|
    68|1. When a client reports treatment is complete, confirm via phone and document the call
    69|2. Trigger the phase change to "Demand in Progress" and log who authorized it
    70|3. Immediately create tasks for any outstanding records/bills requests
    71|4. When records arrive, review them for references to other providers — add any new providers found
    72|5. Separate records by relatedness to the accident
    73|6. Send final health insurance lien requests (to Rawlings, Optum, etc.) to get lien amounts
    74|7. Compile all records, bills, and lien information for the attorney's demand preparation
    75|8. When an adjuster calls proactively, document the contact and provide a general update
    76|9. After demand is sent, create a 30-day follow-up task for adjuster response
    77|
    78|## Pitfalls
    79|
    80|- Moving to demand before all providers are identified (records review may reveal new ones)
    81|- Not separating related vs. unrelated treatment in the medical records
    82|- Forgetting to request health insurance lien amounts before the demand
    83|- Not documenting the client's confirmation that treatment is complete
    84|- Failing to follow up with the adjuster after demand is sent
    85|- Assuming the demand-to-settlement path is linear (client may reject the offer)
    86|
    87|## Related
    88|
    89|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be complete before demand
    90|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — demand is a formal phase change with prerequisites
    91|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — attorney reviews and approves the demand
    92|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — what happens after the demand is sent
    93|", "total_lines": 92, "file_size": 10506, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false, "_budget_warning": "[BUDGET: Iteration 35/50. 15 iterations left. Start consolidating your work.]"}
